behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: The core failure is local verification and branching logic in the webhook handler: recompute HMAC from raw body, compare to header, and short-circuit before enqueue. No real I/O is required to catch this failure.
behaviors.test_description: Call the handler with a known raw body and an invalid `X-Signature-256`; assert HTTP status is 401 and queue-enqueue function is never called.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: This is an ordering guarantee across handler and queue persistence boundary. A unit test with a fake queue can miss real timing/order bugs in actual queue write semantics.
behaviors.test_description: Run the real handler wired to the real queue module (local persistent backend). Instrument queue write completion and response send; assert response 200 is emitted only after write promise resolves/commit completes.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: The failure is in wiring between ingress handler, queue, and async worker trigger. You need multiple modules to verify decoupling; a single-function unit test can miss accidental await/blocking in integration code.
behaviors.test_description: Wire handler + real queue + a downstream processor that intentionally sleeps. Send a valid event and assert response returns before processor completion signal.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: This is a wall-clock SLO under concurrency, sensitive to real HTTP server behavior, event-loop scheduling, and runtime contention. Lower-level tests can hide latency introduced by actual server/network stack behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Start the service process and send concurrent HTTP requests while earlier queued events are processed slowly. Measure end-to-end response time at client side; assert each new webhook response is <3s.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: Deduplication correctness depends on interaction between handler and persistent queue/idempotency store. A pure unit test can miss uniqueness/race behavior at storage boundary.
behaviors.test_description: Using real queue persistence, submit the same event ID twice and assert exactly one queued record exists (or second write returns duplicate outcome without enqueue).

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: This is contract mapping across module boundary: queue-full condition must translate to specific HTTP response and header. Mock-free integration with real queue module catches mismatched error typing/propagation.
behaviors.test_description: Configure real queue module to capacity N and prefill it; send another valid webhook and assert HTTP 503 plus `Retry-After` header is present with expected policy value.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: This validates real capacity enforcement behavior of the persistence/queue runtime itself, which often depends on concrete backend process/driver semantics. Mocks or in-memory substitutes can falsely pass.
behaviors.test_description: Boot the real queue backend used by the service (e.g., DB/Redis process), configure small capacity, enqueue until full, then enqueue one more and assert backend-level rejection is returned.
